cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: What is the Q-value of a nuclear decay?
cards.a: The Q-value is the total energy released by a nuclear decay. It equals the difference in rest-mass energy between the initial and final states.

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: Explain the main nuclear processes occurring in radioactive sources.
cards.a:
• $\alpha$ decay
• $\beta^-$ decay
• $\beta^+$ decay
• Electron capture (EC)
• $\gamma$ emission
• Internal conversion
• Auger electron emission
• Neutron production (spontaneous fission and nuclear reactions)

figure.caption: Overview of the principal radioactive processes. The diagram groups the processes by the interaction responsible (strong, weak, or electromagnetic), shows how they change the nuclear quantum numbers $(A,Z)$, and illustrates the cascade from internal conversion to characteristic X-rays or Auger electrons.

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q:
Compare the principal radioactive processes. For each process, identify:

• the particles emitted,
• the change in $(A,Z)$,
• the typical energy range,
• the main experimental signature.

figure.caption: Overview of the principal radioactive processes.

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: Which emissions are monoenergetic, and which have continuous energy spectra?
cards.a:
Monoenergetic:
• $\alpha$
• $\gamma$
• Internal conversion electrons
• Auger electrons
• Characteristic X-rays
• Annihilation photons

Continuous:
• $\beta^-$
• $\beta^+$
• Neutron spectra from most laboratory sources

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: Order the main radiations by increasing penetrating power.
cards.a: $\alpha$ < Auger electrons < $\beta$ < $\gamma$ < neutrons

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: What are the main laboratory neutron sources, and what are their advantages?
cards.a:
• Spontaneous fission (e.g. $^{252}$Cf): simple, broad neutron spectrum.
• $(\alpha,n)$ sources (e.g. Am-Be): high neutron yield, broad spectrum.
• $(\gamma,n)$ sources: lower neutron yield but nearly monoenergetic neutrons.

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: What is the activity of a radioactive source?
cards.a: The activity is the average number of nuclear disintegrations per unit time. It depends on the amount of radioactive material present.

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: What are the SI and traditional units of radioactive activity?
cards.a: The SI unit is the Becquerel (Bq), where $$1\,\mathrm{Bq}=1\ \mathrm{disintegration/s}.$$ The traditional unit is the Curie (Ci), where $$1\,\mathrm{Ci}=3.7\times10^{10}\ \mathrm{disintegrations/s}.$$

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: What is the difference between activity and dose?
cards.a: Activity (Bq, Ci) measures the rate of radioactive disintegrations in the source. Dose (Gy, Sv) measures the effect of radiation absorbed by matter or by a person.

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: What does the radioactive decay law state?
cards.a: The number of radioactive nuclei (and therefore the activity) decreases exponentially with time: $$N(t)=N_0e^{-\lambda t}.$$

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: What is the physical meaning of the decay constant $\lambda$?
cards.a: The decay constant is the probability per unit time that a nucleus will decay.

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: What are the definitions of the mean lifetime and half-life?
cards.a: The mean lifetime is $$\tau=\frac{1}{\lambda}.$$ The half-life is the time required for the number of radioactive nuclei (or the activity) to decrease to one-half of its initial value.

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: What is the relationship between the half-life and the decay constant?
cards.a: $$T_{1/2}=\frac{\ln2}{\lambda}=\tau\ln2.$$

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: Which probability distribution describes radioactive counting statistics?
cards.a: The number of radioactive decays observed during a fixed counting interval follows a Poisson distribution.

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: For radioactive counting, what are the standard deviation and relative uncertainty?
cards.a: For Poisson statistics, $$\sigma=\sqrt{N}$$ and the relative statistical uncertainty is $$\frac{\sigma}{N}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}.$$

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: Compare $\alpha$ decay and $\beta$ decay.
cards.a: $\alpha$ decay emits a $^4$He nucleus and changes both $A$ and $Z$ ($A-4$, $Z-2$). It is a two-body process and therefore produces monoenergetic particles. $\beta$ decay converts a neutron into a proton (or vice versa) through the weak interaction, leaving $A$ unchanged and changing $Z$ by $\pm1$. It is a three-body process, producing a continuous energy spectrum because part of the energy is carried away by the (anti)neutrino.

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: Compare $\beta^+$ decay and electron capture.
cards.a: Both processes convert a proton into a neutron and produce the same daughter nucleus. In $\beta^+$ decay a positron and a neutrino are emitted, whereas electron capture absorbs an orbital electron and emits only a neutrino. Electron capture can occur even when $\beta^+$ decay is energetically forbidden because no positron has to be created.

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: Compare $\gamma$ emission and internal conversion.
cards.a: Both are modes of nuclear de-excitation. In $\gamma$ emission the nucleus emits a photon. In internal conversion the nucleus transfers its excitation energy directly to an atomic electron, which is ejected. Both processes produce discrete (monoenergetic) radiation determined by the nuclear transition energy.

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: Compare internal conversion electrons and Auger electrons.
cards.a: An internal conversion electron originates from a nuclear transition: the nucleus transfers its excitation energy directly to an atomic electron. An Auger electron originates from an atomic-shell transition: the energy released when an electron fills an inner-shell vacancy is transferred to another atomic electron. Internal conversion electrons typically have energies of hundreds of keV to MeV, whereas Auger electrons usually have energies of only a few keV.

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: Compare spontaneous-fission neutron sources and $(\alpha,n)$ neutron sources.
cards.a: Spontaneous-fission sources (e.g. $^{252}$Cf) produce neutrons directly through nuclear fission and emit several neutrons per fission event. $(\alpha,n)$ sources (e.g. Am-Be) produce neutrons when $\alpha$ particles interact with light nuclei such as Be. Spontaneous-fission sources generally produce higher neutron yields, while $(\alpha,n)$ sources are compact and widely used laboratory neutron sources.

cards.topic: Ch.1: Basic Nuclear Processes in Radioactive Sources
cards.q: Compare activity, radiation output, and dose.
cards.a: Activity measures the number of nuclear disintegrations per unit time (Bq, Ci). Radiation output refers to the emitted particles or photons and depends on the decay scheme. Dose (Gy, Sv) measures the energy deposited or biological effect of the radiation on matter or a person.
